ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for spring chicken with mandi rice. FOODON_03301521 (chicken dish) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (chicken meat, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for spring chicken mandi as a composite dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for spring chicken with mandi rice as a composite dish. SNOMED CT does not index this specific preparation.

serving.description: 1 whole spring chicken with mandi rice (approximately 600 g total: ~300 g chicken meat + ~300 g cooked mandi rice)
serving.natural_language_portion: One serving consists of one whole spring chicken (approximately 300 g edible meat after cooking) served alongside approximately 300 g of aromatic mandi rice, totalling roughly 600 g per portion.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central: Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, cooked, roasted (FDC ID 171477) and Rice, white, long-grain, cooked (FDC ID 169756); composite estimate weighted 50% chicken meat / 50% mandi rice
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for spring chicken with mandi rice exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated as a 50:50 weighted composite of (1) USDA FoodData Central roasted chicken meat only (FDC ID 171477, SR Legacy) and (2) USDA FoodData Central cooked long-grain white rice (FDC ID 169756, SR Legacy), adjusted upward for ghee and spice contribution to fat and sodium. Mandi rice is cooked in spiced broth with ghee, which raises fat and sodium relative to plain boiled rice. Values represent a plausible central estimate for the composite dish per 100 g edible portion.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; white rice (boiled) GI ~64; composite dish GI estimated at 56 reflecting protein and fat co-ingestion attenuating glycemic response
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (2008)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for spring chicken with mandi rice. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled white rice (GI ~64, medium). The composite dish GI is estimated at 56 because co-ingestion of chicken protein (~13.5 g/100 g) and fat (~6.2 g/100 g) is well-documented to attenuate postprandial glycemic response relative to rice alone (Brand-Miller et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (56 × 111 g carbohydrate in 600 g serving) / 100 ≈ 62.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from spiced broth and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Each 600 g serving provides approximately 1860 mg sodium, which represents 81% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size to half a serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from mandi rice component
type2_diabetes.guidance: The mandi rice component contributes a glycemic load of approximately 62 per full serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit rice portion to 150 g and pair with the protein-rich chicken to blunt postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ghee and skin, and elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides approximately 12.6 g saturated fat and 1860 mg sodium per full serving; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose skinless chicken, request ghee-reduced preparation, and limit portion to half a serving to keep saturated fat within AHA guidelines of less than 13 g per day.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spring Chicken with Mandi Rice is a Turkish and Middle Eastern dish featuring a whole young chicken (poussin or spring chicken, typically 400–600 g live weight) that is marinated in a blend of aromatic spices including cumin, cardamom, cinnamon, black pepper, and garlic, then grilled or roasted until the skin is golden and the meat is tender and juicy. The chicken is served atop or alongside mandi rice — a fragrant long-grain basmati rice slow-cooked in seasoned chicken broth enriched with ghee, saffron or turmeric, and whole spices such as bay leaves and cloves. The dish originates from the Levant and Arabian Peninsula culinary tradition and is popular across Turkish, Lebanese, and Gulf Arab restaurants. In Singapore, it is served at Turkish and Lebanese restaurants such as Sultan Palace at Boat Quay. The flavour profile is deeply savoury and aromatic, with the spiced rice absorbing the cooking juices of the chicken. Nutritionally, the dish is protein-rich from the chicken (approximately 13.5 g protein per 100 g composite) and provides moderate carbohydrate from the rice component. The ghee used in mandi rice preparation contributes saturated fat, and the spiced broth adds meaningful sodium. The glycemic index of the composite dish is estimated at 56 (medium), moderated by the protein and fat co-ingestion. Clinically, the dish is of moderate concern for hypertension due to sodium, for Type 2 Diabetes due to the rice glycemic load, and for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat from ghee and chicken skin.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for spring chicken with mandi rice exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or any primary regional database. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for roasted chicken meat and cooked white rice, with adjustments for ghee and sodium from spiced broth. GI is analogously estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled white rice, adjusted for protein and fat co-ingestion. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates (50% roasted chicken meat, 50% mandi rice by edible weight) derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries FDC ID 171477 (chicken, broilers, meat only, roasted) and FDC ID 169756 (rice, white, long-grain, cooked). Fat and sodium values are adjusted upward to account for ghee (~1 tsp per 100 g rice) and salted broth used in mandi preparation. 2. GI is an analogous estimate based on boiled white rice (GI 64, University of Sydney GI Database) attenuated to 56 by protein and fat co-ingestion per Brand-Miller et al. (2008). 3. The dish is served at Sultan Palace, Boat Quay, Singapore, under the menu name '46. Spring Chicken' at SGD 34.90. 4. Halal status is assumed based on the restaurant's Turkish and Lebanese cuisine profile; formal halal certification should be verified with the restaurant. 5. Gluten-free status is assumed as the primary ingredients (chicken, rice, spices) are naturally gluten-free; cross-contamination risk in restaurant kitchens cannot be excluded. 6. Multilingual names for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are provided following Singaporean dish multilingual rules as this dish is served in Singapore, even though the cuisine origin is Turkish. 7. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because they are not consistently reported or reliably calculable from the composite USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy sources used for this estimation: monounsaturated_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source; polyunsaturated_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source; trans_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source; potassium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source; calcium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source; iron_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source; cholesterol_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source; vitamin_c_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source; vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source; vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source; vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source; vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source; vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source; vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source; folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source; vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source (vitamin D content of chicken meat varies with feed and is not consistently captured); vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source; phosphorus_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source; magnesium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source; zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source; selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source; water_g is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source.
